examples.title: Algorithm: Process Creation with fork() and exec()
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating the process creation mechanism using fork() and exec() system calls
examples.code:
ALGORITHM ProcessCreation
INPUT: ProgramName (string), ProgramArgs (list)
OUTPUT: None

BEGIN
 // Step 1: Fork the current process
 pid := FORK()

 // Step 2: Check fork result
 IF pid < 0 THEN
 // Fork failed
 PRINT "Error: Process creation failed"
 EXIT with error code
 END IF

 IF pid == 0 THEN
 // This is the CHILD process
 PRINT "Child: I am the child process"
 PRINT "Child: My PID is", GET_CURRENT_PID()
 PRINT "Child: Replacing program with", ProgramName

 // Step 3: Child replaces its image with new program
 EXEC(ProgramName, ProgramArgs)

 // If exec returns, it failed
 PRINT "Error: Exec failed"
 EXIT with error code
 ELSE
 // This is the PARENT process
 PRINT "Parent: I am the parent process"
 PRINT "Parent: Child PID is", pid

 // Step 4: Parent waits for child to complete
 PRINT "Parent: Waiting for child..."
 status := WAIT(pid)

 // Step 5: Child has completed
 PRINT "Parent: Child has terminated with status", status
 PRINT "Parent: Resuming execution"
 END IF

 PRINT "Program completed successfully"
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode illustrates the two-step process creation in UNIX systems: 1) fork() creates a child process that is a copy of the parent, 2) exec() replaces the child's memory space with a new program. The parent uses wait() to synchronize with the child.

examples.title: Python: Process Creation and Management
examples.description: Python implementation demonstrating process creation using the multiprocessing module
examples.code:
import multiprocessing
import os
import time

def child_process_function(number):
 """Function executed by child process"""
 pid = os.getpid()
 print(f"[Child PID {pid}] Starting computation: {number} * 2 = {number * 2}")
 time.sleep(1) # Simulate some work
 print(f"[Child PID {pid}] Computation complete")
 return number * 2

def demonstrate_process_creation():
 """Demonstrate process creation using multiprocessing"""
 parent_pid = os.getpid()
 print(f"Parent Process PID: {parent_pid}")
 print("=" * 50)

 # Create a new process
 process = multiprocessing.Process(
 target=child_process_function,
 args=(10,)
 )

 # Start the child process
 print("Starting child process...")
 process.start()

 # Parent continues execution
 print(f"Parent: Child process started with PID: {process.pid}")
 print("Parent: Doing other work while child runs...")

 # Wait for child to complete
 print("Parent: Waiting for child to finish...")
 process.join()

 print(f"Parent: Child process finished (alive={process.is_alive()})")
 print("=" * 50)

def demonstrate_process_pool():
 """Demonstrate creating multiple child processes"""
 print("\n--- Creating Process Pool ---")

 with multiprocessing.Pool(processes=3) as pool:
 numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
 results = pool.map(child_process_function, numbers)

 print(f"Results: {results}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
 demonstrate_process_creation()
 demonstrate_process_pool()

 # Demonstrate orphan and zombie concepts
 print("\n--- Demonstrating Process States ---")
 p = multiprocessing.Process(target=time.sleep, args=(2,))
 p.start()
 print(f"Process {p.pid} state: Started (will become zombie after join)")
 p.join()
 print(f"Process {p.pid} state: Terminated and joined (resources freed)")
examples.explanation: Python's multiprocessing module provides a high-level interface for process management. This example shows: 1) Creating processes with the Process class, 2) Starting processes with start(), 3) Waiting for completion with join(), 4) Using process pools for parallel work. The multiprocessing model abstracts the underlying fork() system call.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for n processes (each process has its own memory space)

examples.title: C: Process Creation with fork(), exec(), and wait()
examples.description: C implementation demonstrating UNIX process management system calls
examples.explanation: This C program demonstrates the fundamental UNIX process management system calls: 1) fork() - creates a child process (duplicate of parent), 2) exec() family - replaces child process image with new program, 3) wait()/waitpid() - parent synchronizes with child termination. The first function shows fork() returning 0 to child and child's PID to parent. The second shows how exec() replaces the child's program while maintaining the same PID.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for fork and exec, O(t) for wait where t is child execution time
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is the size of the parent process address space (copy-on-write)

examples.title: C: Zombie and Orphan Process Handling
examples.description: C code demonstrating zombie and orphan process concepts and the role of wait()
examples.explanation: This program demonstrates critical process lifecycle concepts: 1) Zombie processes - occur when a child terminates but parent hasn't called wait(); the process table entry (PCB) remains until parent collects exit status, 2) Orphan processes - occur when parent terminates before child; children are reparented to init (PID 1) which calls wait() for them. Understanding these states is crucial for proper resource management and preventing system resource leaks.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for process creation/termination, O(t) for wait() where t is child execution time
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) per zombie (just the PCB), O(1) per orphan process
